questions.subject: Environment
questions.explanation: All four communities listed—Raika, Rabari, Fakirani Jat, and Manganiyar—are traditional pastoralist communities in India known for camel-rearing.
questions.takeaway: The Raika, Rabari, Fakirani Jat, and Manganiyar are key pastoralist communities associated with rearing camels in the dryland ecosystems of India.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Camel-rearing Pastoralist communities: Raika, Rabari, Fakirani Jat, and Manganiyar...

questions.subject: Environment
questions.prompt: In the context of India's cultural and ecological heritage, the traditional communities known as 'Raika', 'Rabari', and 'Fakirani Jat' are primarily recognized as:

options.text: Nomadic pastoralists traditionally engaged in camel rearing in the dryland ecosystems of western India.
Forest-dwelling communities practicing shifting cultivation in the central Indian highlands.
Artisanal communities specializing in the weaving of Pashmina shawls in the trans-Himalayan region.
Traditional fisherfolk utilizing sustainable estuarine fishing techniques along the Gujarat coast.

questions.explanation: The Raika, Rabari, Fakirani Jat, and Manganiyar are traditional camel-rearing pastoralist communities primarily located in the dryland ecosystems of Rajasthan and Gujarat.
questions.takeaway: The Raika, Rabari, and Fakirani Jat are key pastoralist groups historically associated with camel rearing and dryland ecology in India.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Camel-rearing Pastoralist communities: Raika, Rabari, Fakirani Jat, and Manganiyar...
